title: Why Modern Women Are 'Out of Balance' and How to Return to Your Feminine Nature
guestBio: Chelsea Ann O'Brien is a board-certified family nurse practitioner and author of five books including The Feminine Effect and Mystical Motherhood. She's a modern healer who studied with renowned midwife Ina May Gaskin and practices at The Morrison Center in NYC, dedicated to helping women remember their true nature.
excerpt: Chelsea Ann reveals why modern women are burning out and losing touch with their intuition, sharing her journey from extreme wealth to spiritual surrender and the profound shift she sees coming in 2027.
metaDescription: Discover why modern women are 'out of balance' and Chelsea Ann's path back to feminine nature, healing burnout and reconnecting with intuition.
keyTakeaways: Modern women are 'out of their nature' due to conditioning that pushes them into masculine energy patterns.
The father wound drives who you date, while the mother wound drives how you mother your children.
Motherhood isn't an identity to grasp but a gift that exists beyond the ego.
Healing requires feeling your emotions, not thinking your way through them.
Women don't need to become like men to access their power and succeed.
Returning to feminine nature starts with creating space from societal conditioning.
Redefining success means basing it on your authentic nature rather than external validation.

pullQuotes.text: Women don't need to become like men to be powerful
Motherhood isn't an identity — it's a gift that exists beyond ego
Healing requires feeling — you cannot think your way to an open heart

timestamps.label: Introduction — Motherhood, Feminine Energy & Returning to Nature
Motherhood as the Deepest Growth Experience
EMDR, Subconscious Programming & the Mother Wound
The 2027 Shift — Why Women Are Losing Intuition
Feminine Amnesia — 2,000 Years of Erased Knowledge
Surrender & the Spiritual Journey to Egypt
The Noble Woman — Everything Without Losing Family
AI, the Future & Returning to Authenticity
The Original Wound — Father Wound & Dating
Leave Your Island — Advice for Disconnected Women

content:
If you've been feeling burned out, disconnected from your intuition, or like you're living someone else's version of success, you're not alone. According to Chelsea Ann O'Brien, nurse practitioner, author, and modern healer, modern women are experiencing what she calls being "out of their nature" — and it's wreaking havoc on our health, relationships, and sense of purpose.

In this profound conversation with Aimee, Chelsea shares her extraordinary journey from living a billionaire lifestyle to surrendering everything after a spiritual awakening that took her from the Dalai Lama's temple to Egypt's Great Pyramid. Her story isn't just personal transformation — it's a roadmap for women who are ready to return to their authentic feminine nature.

The Crisis of Modern Womanhood

Chelsea doesn't mince words when describing what she sees happening to women today. We've been conditioned to operate in masculine energy patterns — pushing, striving, grinding through 70-hour work weeks — and our bodies and spirits are paying the price. This isn't about women being weak; it's about being fundamentally out of alignment with our natural rhythms and gifts.

The conditioning runs deep, starting with what Chelsea identifies as the mother wound and father wound. The father wound drives who you date, while the mother wound drives how you mother. These unconscious patterns shape our relationships and parenting in ways we rarely recognize, passing generational trauma down the line until someone breaks the cycle.

What makes this particularly challenging is that we've lost access to what Chelsea calls our "genetic gift" of intuition. Where women once relied on their inner knowing to navigate life's complexities, we've been taught to override that wisdom with external validation, manipulation, and masculine-coded achievement metrics.

The Path Back to Feminine Nature

Chelsea's own awakening came through the ultimate surrender — letting go of extreme wealth, status, and the identity she'd built around external success. Her journey took her to Egypt, where she experienced what she describes as a "heart-opening" process that fundamentally shifted how she understood feminine power.

This isn't about rejecting ambition or success. It's about redefining success on your own terms rather than chasing what Chelsea calls "dangling carrots" that keep you perpetually striving but never fulfilled. When you return to your feminine nature, you access a different kind of power — one that doesn't require you to become like men to be effective.

The healing process Chelsea describes isn't intellectual. You cannot think your way to an open heart. It requires feeling your emotions, processing your wounds, and creating space from the conditioning that's kept you disconnected from your authentic self. This might involve modalities like EMDR, plant medicine, or other heart-opening practices that allow suppressed emotions to move through your system.

Motherhood Beyond Identity

One of the most powerful insights Chelsea shares is about motherhood itself. In our culture, we've turned motherhood into an identity to grasp and defend, but Chelsea sees it differently. Motherhood isn't an identity — it's a gift that exists beyond ego. When you approach motherhood from this perspective, it becomes less about performing the role perfectly and more about allowing the experience to transform you.

This understanding extends to how we think about epigenetics during pregnancy. The emotional and spiritual state you're in while pregnant literally shapes your baby's genetic expression and future health. This isn't about creating more pressure for pregnant women — it's about recognizing the profound opportunity pregnancy offers for healing generational patterns.

The Coming Shift

Chelsea predicts a massive shift coming in 2027, where more women will begin remembering their true nature and stepping away from the masculine-dominated paradigms that have defined success for the past several decades. This isn't wishful thinking — she sees it already happening as women increasingly burn out from trying to be everything to everyone while losing themselves in the process.

The future Chelsea envisions isn't about women retreating from leadership or impact. It's about leading from a place of authentic feminine power — the kind that doesn't require you to sacrifice your family, health, or inner wisdom to make a difference in the world. She calls this the "noble woman" archetype: someone who can have everything without losing what matters most.

Creating Your Own Return

If you're ready to begin your own return to feminine nature, Chelsea's advice is clear: start by creating space from the conditioning that's been running your life. This might mean stepping back from the 70-hour work weeks, questioning whose version of success you've been chasing, or simply beginning to listen to your body's wisdom again.

The path isn't always comfortable. As Chelsea puts it, your heart has to break before it opens. But on the other side of that breaking is access to your authentic power — the kind that doesn't require you to betray your nature to be effective in the world.

You don't have to choose between being powerful and being feminine. You don't have to sacrifice your family for your career or your career for your family. The integration is possible, but it requires you to stop trying to do it the way men do it and start honoring the unique gifts that come with your feminine nature.

This conversation with Chelsea Ann isn't just about healing — it's about remembering who you really are beneath all the conditioning and expectations. It's about coming home to yourself so you can create the life and legacy that truly align with your deepest values and gifts.

quiz.title: What's Your Feminine Energy Archetype?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally express your feminine power and where you might be operating out of alignment with your authentic nature.

questions.question: When you're facing a big decision, what do you typically do first?

options.text: Trust your gut feeling and let the answer come to you in quiet moments
Create a pros and cons list and research all possible outcomes
Consider how it will affect everyone you care about before thinking of yourself
Ask yourself what your authentic self would choose, not what others expect

questions.question: How do you feel about the idea of 'having it all' as a modern woman?

options.text: It feels overwhelming - you'd rather focus on what truly matters to your soul
It's absolutely possible with the right strategy and work ethic
It sounds exhausting - someone always ends up sacrificing when you try to do everything
The whole concept feels like society's definition, not yours

questions.question: When you think about your relationship with your mother, what comes up?

options.text: Deep empathy for her journey and the wounds she carried
Appreciation for how she taught you to be strong and independent
Recognition of patterns you've inherited that you're working to heal
Awareness that her path doesn't have to define your own

questions.question: What does success look like to you right now?

options.text: Being of service and helping others heal and grow
Achieving your goals while maintaining high standards in everything you do
Creating a loving, stable environment for the people in your care
Living authentically according to your own values, regardless of external validation

questions.question: How do you typically handle strong emotions?

options.text: You feel them deeply and often need space to process the intensity
You analyze what triggered them and make a plan to address the situation
You often put them aside to take care of others first
You're learning to sit with them instead of immediately trying to fix or escape

questions.question: What draws you most about the idea of embracing your feminine nature?

options.text: The permission to trust your intuition as a valid source of wisdom
Finding a sustainable way to achieve without burning out
Healing generational patterns so you can show up differently for others
The freedom to define power and success on your own terms

results.title: The Intuitive Healer
results.description: You have a deep connection to your inner wisdom and naturally sense what others need. You're drawn to healing work and creating sacred spaces for transformation. While you honor your intuitive gifts, you may sometimes struggle with boundaries or feel overwhelmed by others' energy. This episode will help you understand how to protect your sensitive nature while stepping fully into your healing power.

results.title: The Driven Achiever
results.description: You're ambitious, goal-oriented, and capable of incredible things when you set your mind to it. Your determination has likely brought you success, but you may find yourself exhausted from constantly pushing forward. You might be operating heavily in masculine energy patterns without realizing it. This episode will show you how to harness your natural drive while reconnecting with your feminine flow and intuition.

results.title: The Sacred Mother
results.description: Whether or not you have children, you naturally nurture and care for others with deep compassion. You understand that mothering is about creating and tending to life in all its forms. You may struggle with putting yourself first or recognizing your own needs. This episode will help you explore how your mother wound might be influencing your relationships and discover how to mother from a place of wholeness rather than depletion.

results.title: The Awakening Seeker
results.description: You sense there's something deeper calling you beyond society's expectations, but you're not sure what it is yet. You feel pulled toward spiritual growth and authentic living, even when it doesn't make logical sense. You're ready to question everything you've been taught about success and power. This episode will validate your inner knowing and provide a roadmap for returning to your true feminine nature.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Motherhood, feminine energy, returning to nature. These are words that mean very different things to different women. And today, we're going there. But before we begin today's conversation, I want to ground us into something I talk about often here at The Manager. So many women have been taught to lead like a ladder, to push harder, to move faster, and measure their worth by how much they carry. But what I've learned through motherhood, through business, through life from the inside out is that real leadership often looks more like a garden. It's cyclical, it's intuitive, and sometimes the most powerful growth happens when we return to the parts of ourselves we were told to outgrow.

Today's guest, Chelsea Ann, explores the idea through a deeply spiritual and feminine lens. She's an author of five books centered around motherhood, energy, and what she describes as returning women to their nature, inviting us to examine the wounds, stories, and identities that shape how we show up in the world. Now, as always on this podcast, this space is not about telling women who to be. It's about expanding the conversation, holding nuance, curiosity, and respect for the paths that many women walk. Because whether you lead a company, a home, or a community, or simply your own healing, your inner world is part of your leadership.

Chelsea, welcome to The Manager Podcast. It's so nice to have you here.

**Chelsea:** Oh, it's so nice to have you. You know, I have to say, right when you started, the window randomly opened, the energy just filled the room. And I was like, oh no, right now. And it's closed and it just opened itself. So it's going to be a really powerful podcast. Thanks for having me. And happy you're here. Beautiful intro.

**Aimee:** Oh, thank you. I'm so excited. There's so much crossover in our work and our curiosity about the human experience. You know, I feel like we both have such a curious approach to our stories, to our wounds and all of these things. And it was like right away we had this thing when we talked for the first time. It was like I've known you forever. That's what it felt like.

**Chelsea:** Yeah, I felt like that, too, for me. And I haven't felt that a long time unless it's a soul family member. So that's really nice. Thank you. The window closed. OK, it's confirming us. I love that. And I feel like we connected on a soul level over motherhood.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Yeah, motherhood and, you know, the experience. Motherhood is one of those profoundly life-changing and deeply moving experiences. It pushes you harder than you've ever dreamed you would be pushed in life. You know, it pushes you to the absolute edge of your ability. You didn't know you could hold your pee for that long. Yeah. You didn't know you could go that long without washing your hair or sleeping or caring for yourself. I mean, motherhood is one of those things that really does, yeah, absolutely pushes you and kind of makes you become, it pushes you, it grows you into becoming a mother.

**Chelsea:** To yourself. Yeah. And to all the wounds that you needed to heal for the generations if you choose to do it and be a good one. Absolutely. Yeah, you have a choice. At the end of my book, The Feminine Effect, Discovering Your True Nature, one of the last parts of it. You're going to love the end when you get to it. But I realize everything had been taken away from me during this transformational journey where I returned to my femininity and I was sitting on the beach and all I had left is everything was just gone. I was just sitting with my kids.

And that's when I actually met my current husband is in this moment where I was in just motherhood. And I noted in the book, motherhood isn't an identity anymore. For those who's listening, we'll talk about it, but the story is about all the things we have to let go of in the outside world through society and programming and everything that we cling to, the carrot in the air that we're always looking for the next thing to fulfill us. I went through a process of really just having everything stripped from me.

I know that we all have to go through this if you haven't gone through it already, but all I was left with in this moment was motherhood. And I realized that that wasn't an identity. God gave me that. Yes. It was like, that's what God gives you as a woman. I don't really know what else God gives a woman, but the ability to reproduce. I mean, that sounds, I'm going to make people mad, but it really, well, it's true. That's your job it is you know it is true it's.

**Aimee:** It really is this thing. If we're just looking at humanity from a biological standpoint, you know, if humans were animals, you know, which we are, if we were looking at us and we are just the zookeeper and we're caring for these animals, you know, the female of the human species would be the one who, yes, carries the young in her womb, makes the milk. We are mammals, you know, and that's our, biologically, that is a very important job for humanity because it's the procreation of the species itself.

**Chelsea:** And how it's done is the most important decision, right? And the way you consciously create and the way that you program the baby and the things you allow and your frequency, which is what the work is all about that I do.

**Aimee:** Yeah, absolutely. I would, you know, I recently did some EMDR. I did an intensive EMDR session in about a little over a month ago. And the stuff that came up, I was working on current issues and what came up for me was childhood programming. And, you know, when you dig deep, and they hide. It's hiding so deep in your subconscious mind, you know? And it was really what was holding me back from having the kind of relationship I've always wanted to have with my partner, my husband.

You know, I was just having an argument with my sister about something. I'll say it out loud to everybody because I have an open book. But I said to her something, and I didn't mean it so black and white, but she took it that way. But I said therapy doesn't fully work. I think therapy works. And I think it gets to the top of the consciousness. And then there's the subconscious. And then, so I've written tons of books on different ways to get into the subconscious. And a lot of people know what I'm talking about, but EMDR is one of them.

But if you don't go into the subconscious programming of mom and dad, that is how you become a good mother and a good wife and a good family person. If you don't heal mom wound, if you don't heal dad wound, I mean, a lot of the book you're reading, The Feminine Effect, is about the dad wound. And then there's the mother wound is a totally different beast.

**Chelsea:** Totally. Yeah. This was a mother wound and I didn't even know I had it. And I was standing in my blind spot all these years just thinking, you know, I guess I can do everything else, but I can't do this. I felt unworthy and I felt insignificant. And how can you be a significant other if you feel insignificant? That's why my marriages had failed.

That was really, that was the part of me that was contributing to the failure of my marriage was my inability to truly deeply love myself and feel worthy enough that I could adequately represent my needs in the relationship so that my partner knew how to stand up and do what he needed to do for me. Right. Because then you keep creating fights so that you can always feel that you're insignificant and then blame it on him.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. Or, you know, it's just silly things like, you know, if you don't feel like washing the dishes, it's OK to say, hey, you know, I can't do it tonight. I need someone else. I tap out someone else needs to do this. But there's this unworthiness that says, no, you know, you made these children and this is your burden. And there's this, you know, that Eve story that comes in that you don't even know you're carrying.

**Chelsea:** The Eve story. You know, that was in that book, too. And we do, we carry a lot of it. It's, and it's honestly, it's, I've had this discussion with other women on my show and it's very interesting how the epigenetics of this stuff, how we're, we have germ cells and we carry some of the memories of our ancestors in our actual DNA.

You get into this stuff. I wrote a whole book on epigenetics called Fertile. And there's only one organization, it's called APPA, that looks at women's reprogramming epigenetics during pregnancy. And I thought, why is nobody writing about this? And so it's called Fertile. And I look at, I mean, I look at the elements within the book. I mean, it's funny that I'm always talking about nature, returning to our nature, returning to our nature. But part of it is you have to go through the elements within you that are in balance, fire, earth, air, water, ether. And that's a good way. It's a good theory to begin to approach epigenetics, in my opinion.

**Aimee:** Yeah. No, I got into epigenetics back in 2012. My mother, I had started making this stuff called Momalicious, and it was a prenatal protein powder. It was based on an old recipe by Adele Davis, a nutritionist from the 1960s. My mother had handed me her book when I got married, Let's Have Healthy Children, and I started modernizing her pep-up formula. And that company got picked up and the product got picked up. We got funded. My mom was like, check out these papers. And epigenetics was just coming out.

And so while we were formulating Mommalicious, we were looking at the University of Utah, the study epigenetics on University of Utah website. And so we put all of the different things, butyrate and broccoli aerial parts for the sulforaphane and resveratrol and all of these things that we're going to biologically impact the baby, the epigenetics of the baby. Because there's only, yeah epigenetics can only really be changed in utero.

**Chelsea:** It is so funny. I included those in my book, those supplements. And I didn't even I was guided by spirit to put them in, but I didn't know. I was like, are you sure? I have a whole list for you. I have a whole list. I can give you my formulation. It's no longer being manufactured. My company got bought out in 2017 by a big Chinese company. And they shelved it, though. So it's now there's no product on the market for moms.

And they do. Now you can't. really think this is so necessary because my mother was born with, my mother has spina bifida, which could have been prevented in utero, but we carry MTFHR. And her mom already had a terrible diet to begin with. So folic acid can't be converted into folate. We can't do it. We don't have the enzyme to convert it. So we started there.

Had my mother been given adequate folate during her gestation, she would have been born perfectly formed, she wouldn't have had her birth defect and she wouldn't have had that pain for her whole life that she's had her whole life. She has a hole in her spine. And for us, it was like, well, what else could we prevent? What other things are going on in there that are preventable that we can optimize these babies, their epigenetics?

There's so many. But then there's the whole emotional aspect of epigenetics too, the stress that mom has while she's pregnant. You know, the sleep, the thoughts, the emotions, the environment. I mean, I even went deep into what's, what are you looking at all day? What's the sound? What are the colors? What are you wearing? You know, and that's the only way to get to frequency is what is, what are you identified with? Who are you surrounding yourself with? You know, what's your frequency? It's always, what's your frequency?

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. I was just reading a Tesla quote and he was like, if you want to get to the nature of everything, you know, you have to look at frequency. And it's so true. I just got the Bexley frequency machine for our room and I just kind of set it to the frequency of Earth. And that's we sleep with it on at the frequency of Earth. You feel it. I love it. The peace and the calm. I don't know. I actually think I might get one of those. My friend told me about that. That's cool.

**Chelsea:** I think it's really. Yeah, I really dig. I really think frequency is kind of it's funny, but it really could be the future of medicine. You know, I really think that there's a lot more to it. I worked at a clinic in Manhattan. I worked at a clinic in Manhattan. We used it a lot. That's so cool. So what kind of doctor are you? Tell me all about the clinical side of you.

**Aimee:** Okay, let's get into it. I'm not a doctor. I'm a nurse practitioner. I'm pretty advanced now, different studies. We could start with the book, which is the story in itself. But as a practitioner, I studied reproductive medicine, integrative medicine. studied with Ina May Gaskin. And so a lot of midwives, I did labor and delivery for many years. And so I realized we had a lot of problems the way that my passion is really to help women become better mothers, because I think that we are the problem and the solution.

And I have found that women are out of their nature. And so let's take it down to a practical level of just seeing women. Now I'm doing menopause work. So now I'm doing, I do transitions for women. And now I'm becoming an expert in menopause. It's what I do all day long. I love it. Love it, love it, love it.

**Chelsea:** We have to talk about this on my show because it's Oh, I could do another. Menopause is a whole subject for itself and I could do a whole lesson on it. Oh, I would love that. We would love that. I'm answering for every woman who's listening right now. We're all kind of in it and there is not enough information for us. I feel like I have a lot. Yes.

**Aimee:** One of my deep understanding of the transformational process that I went through in The Feminine Effect, and then I wrote again about it in Feminine Frequency coming out in March. If someone's coming to me into the clinic and they have an autoimmune issue, or they have an abusive relationship, or they've gone through a divorce, or their gut's out of hand, or they have eating disorder, or they're working 90 hours a week and they're not with us. There's so many things, okay? And they're out of balance. So they're out of their nature.

And what does that mean? We're getting sick because we've been, not just because of the food we're eating, not just because, it's not just as simple. It's that we have decided as a species that we are, we think that we're being, we're creating evolution, that we're advancing. And I don't think we are. I think as a species, women are actually not really seeing the power of being a female. We have lost our intuition. We have lost our ability to connect to spirit deeply. We have lost our power of our hearts, of our deep knowing.

And we've taken on these characteristics of manipulation and entitlement and jealousy and envy and this distorted reality. And we're passing it on through generation through generation, epigenetically. Let me give you an example of, you know, if we look at an apple orchard, let's go back into nature because we spoke about air, earth, water, you know, ether. All my books are connected, actually.

And so if you look at nature and balance and with when you're really fertile and when things are really healthy, if you have a healthy apple orchard, is there's one tree in the apple orchard that's sick, the owner, if it wasn't producing healthy fruit, if it was getting all the apple trees around it sick, the owner wouldn't feel bad about cutting it down and taking it out. But right now you know if women are apple trees, and we're going to eve you know if women are apple trees, we're sick. We're sick and we're not seeing that.

I do think a lot of the divorces that are created are because we, there's two men in the household. And I do think if women were more in their hearts, we could take men out of greed. And I know that if you go more into your femininity, you can heal. I think it's really going to hit in 2027. And I do feel like a shift just happened over Christmas. And I don't know if you have a bunch of intuitive women listening, but if you felt that shift, if you don't want to do anything anymore.

It's about to hit by 2027, September 2027. Mark my words and mark this in your save it. Because you will not want to enter the world in the same way. And a lot of the identities that you've been clinging on to are not going to work as the frequency changes. And you're not going to understand what's changing anymore. but it's that you can no longer look to society to define who you are as a woman. And you need to figure out what that authenticity is, even if it's just as simple as, I mean, I'm going to go back to just being a mom.

And if we can make that cool again, when did that become not cool? It's so sad. When did we lose that? Why can't we be proud as women just to say, I'm not doing anything. I'm just a mom. But I don't know moms that are happy anymore. And it's, I mean, I can go on about this for 10 hours and I've written so many books on it. So you go ahead and ask me questions.

**Chelsea:** I love it. I mean, there's so much there. Oh, my God. There's meat on the bone everywhere. I mean, I do think we absolutely have. I feel like. OK, so when I was in film school, I was doing a documentary on women, a woman in film. And I was interviewing all these people from L.A., old school Hollywood people. And Haskell, I was interviewing Haskell Wexler and he said, Amy, women need. To learn how to tell stories and make films in a woman's way.

And I thought to myself, what an interesting thing to say. Are there two ways of doing things? Yes. That's what we've forgotten. Yeah. That's what we've forgotten. Yeah. And I realized, looking at my mother growing up, my mother is an amazingly intense, brilliant person. And she was entrepreneur of the year in 1988. But that woman would work just as hard as a man until she burned herself out and ended up in bed for months.

And it was, you know, watching her burn, burn, burn, and then burn out. When he said that to me, I realized... She was imitating a man and maybe everyone's imitating a man. Maybe we're not supposed to. We're not supposed to do it. We've gotten it wrong.

**Aimee:** And, and I, in my next book, the feminine frequency, I pinpoint, I'm going to make everybody a little mad here and then I'm going to go back and heal it. Okay. But in feminism is a great thing. I am, I'm financially independent. I do really well. I am all those things. I my houses and my bank account and my purses and whatever. Right. But feminism was defined by men and it was defined by success was defined by, well, independence at the time was defined by how, you know, how much money you could make.

So if women wanted to become independent, they had to become financially independent to become successful. And so successful success is now defined by, you know, how much money you make. And now women are making so much more money than men. We don't even want to be with men because we make more than them. And so now it's uncomfortable. It's become uncomfortable. We still want to be with men, but now we don't know how to be with men. But because we're in a power struggle.

**Chelsea:** Yes. But I want to bring this back to, okay, Ina May Gaskin, we brought her up in the beginning. Yeah. And I went and I lived on her farm. If anybody doesn't know who she is, she's a spiritual midwife. Okay. And she's probably one of, she is the most famous, famous midwife that's ever lived. And she did something for women that's really never been done. She saved women. natural birth and home birth.

And so I went and I held her hand as her movie was coming out. And it was the first showing of her movie. And she sat next to me. I don't know why. She grabbed my hand the whole movie. And I felt like I was getting a transference or something. And I left that place and my whole life changed after that. And she's a spiritual teacher or something, but she wouldn't know it. I went there to learn to birth. I was doing birth work. And then she held my hand.

And what I realized what Ina May Gaskin did is she, we had almost forgotten what natural birth was. So down in Brazil, it's just C-sections. I think it's up to probably 99% C-sections. So before it was about 96% or 94. They don't know how to naturally birth in Brazil. There's, if no one knows how to do it anymore, it's been a forgotten art. Everyone just gets a C-section. It's an automatic thing. There's very many, there's really not a doctor that can do it there as far as I know, unless it's changing.

So she wrote down in history, don't forget your power. Don't forget who you are. Don't forget. And these books, what I've written, Feminine Effects and Feminine Frequency, is the next one really takes it step by step in 27 chapters for 2027. But it's don't forget you're a woman. If somebody doesn't remind you, we're going to go down such a path that we can't remember.

**Chelsea:** Right. Yes, we have forgotten so much. I actually have worked on a screenplay with my writing partner since 2017, and it's all about what we've forgotten. You know, it's the amnesia, the feminine amnesia, the fact that history was written by the victor and it's his story. It's not our story. So we don't even know who we are, what we're supposed to be. We've lost so much of feminine culture and feminine knowledge and the intuition and what it means to be a woman.

So much of it has been erased in the last 2,000 years. And I really don't think that we as a culture have even begun to approach the wound of that, of what it means to have had so much of our knowledge erased. Because I'm a home birther. I've had five babies in my living room. And to be able to have that experience of really reclaiming something that's very primal, very profoundly transformational, psychedelic almost in the way that you are so between the worlds.
